file_name: XACCT Technologies, Inc.SUPPORT AND MAINTENANCE AGREEMENT.txt

perturbation.original_text: Fees shall be payable within thirty (30) days of invoice which shall be exclusive of any applicable local, state, federal, use, excise, value added, GST or other taxes imposed on the fees payable for the Product Maintenance and Support Services which shall be the responsibility of Licensee.
perturbation.changed_text: Fees shall be payable within ninety (90) days of invoice which shall be exclusive of any applicable local, state, federal, use, excise, value added, GST or other taxes imposed on the fees payable for the Product Maintenance and Support Services which shall be the responsibility of Licensee.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment terms to 90 days could violate California's Prompt Payment laws, depending on the specific industry and context, as certain sectors have shorter legal payment deadlines. California law sets specific requirements for payment to contractors and subcontractors, and a 90-day payment window might conflict with these regulations if XACCT is contracting with a California-based licensee that qualifies for protection under the Prompt Payment laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 7107
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 7107 addresses the failure to make timely payment to subcontractors. While the agreement is not directly a construction agreement, a payment term of 90 days might conflict with the requirement for prompt payment to contractors and subcontractors, depending on the exact nature of the services being provided under the agreement and whether the licensee qualifies as a contractor or subcontractor under California law.

PROFESSIONS AND VOCATIONS GENERALLY [5000 - 9998.11] ( Heading of Division 3 added by Stats. 1939, Ch. 30. ) CHAPTER 9. Contractors [7000 - 7191] ( Chapter 9 added by Stats. 1939, Ch. 37. ) ARTICLE 7. Disciplinary Proceedings [7090 - 7124.6] ( Article 7 added by Stats. 1939, Ch. 37. ) 7107. Abandonment without legal excuse of any construction project or operation engaged in or undertaken by the licensee as a contractor constitutes a cause for disciplinary action. (Added by Stats. 1939, Ch. 37.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the payment term to 90 days, which, according to the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation, could conflict with California's Prompt Payment laws, particularly if the licensee qualifies as a contractor or subcontractor. The original text specified 30 days. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not directly address payment timelines, it references California Business and Professions Code (BPC) and mentions disciplinary proceedings for contractors (BPC 7107). The change to 90 days creates an inconsistency with potential prompt payment requirements, especially if the services fall under contractor/subcontractor regulations within California, thereby making the changed text create a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Agreement will be automatically renewed for additional one (1) year terms (subject to applicable fee adjustments) unless thirty (30) days prior to the anniversary of the Effective Date Licensee gives written notice to XACCT of its intention not to renew.
perturbation.changed_text: The Agreement will be automatically renewed for additional one (1) year terms (subject to applicable fee adjustments) unless ten (10) days prior to the anniversary of the Effective Date Licensee gives written notice to XACCT of its intention not to renew.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notification period for non-renewal to 10 days may violate California's automatic renewal law, which requires a longer notification period for certain types of contracts. This is because the licensee may not get ample time to cancel the renewal, which is a violation of consumer protection laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 17602
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 17602 mandates specific disclosures and cancellation procedures for automatic renewal or continuous service offers. Shortening the notification period to 10 days may not provide the consumer with sufficient time to review and cancel the renewal, thereby violating the statute. This applies if the agreement is characterized as an automatic renewal or continuous service offer.

GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 3. REPRESENTATIONS TO THE PUBLIC [17500 - 17943] ( Part 3 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 63. ) CHAPTER 1. Advertising [17500 - 17606] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 63. ) ARTICLE 9. Automatic Purchase Renewals [17600 - 17606] ( Article 9 added by Stats. 2009, Ch. 350, Sec. 1. ) 17602. (a) It is unlawful for any business that makes an automatic renewal offer or continuous service offer to a consumer in this state to do any of the following: (1) Fail to present the automatic renewal offer terms or continuous service offer terms in a clear and conspicuous manner before the subscription or purchasing agreement is fulfilled and in visual proximity, or, in the case of an offer conveyed by voice, in temporal proximity, to the request for consent to the offer. If the offer also includes a free gift or trial, the offer shall include a clear and conspicuous explanation of the price that will be charged after the trial ends or the manner in which the subscription or purchasing agreement pricing will change upon conclusion of the trial. perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the notification period from 30 days to 10 days, which contradicts California Business and Professions Code Section 17602 if the agreement is an automatic renewal offer or continuous service offer. The law snippet states that it is unlawful for a business making an automatic renewal offer to a consumer to fail to present the terms in a clear and conspicuous manner, suggesting the consumer needs sufficient time to review and cancel. Reducing the notification period to 10 days may not provide this sufficient time, thus contradicting the statute.

perturbation.original_text: XACCT may also terminate Support Services if Licensee breaches any provision of this Agreement or the License Agreement and such breach is not remedied within thirty (30) days after Licensee receives written notice of the breach.
perturbation.changed_text: XACCT may also terminate Support Services if Licensee breaches any provision of this Agreement or the License Agreement, effective immediately after Licensee receives written notice of the breach.
perturbation.explanation: Eliminating the 30-day cure period for breaches could conflict with general contract law principles that provide a reasonable opportunity to cure a breach before termination. California law generally favors allowing parties a reasonable time to cure breaches, especially if the breach is not material.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 1442 implies a right to cure a breach before a forfeiture (such as termination) can be enforced. By making termination effective immediately upon notice of breach, the modified text eliminates this right to cure, potentially conflicting with California law. Although relating to lease conditions, it illustrates the general principle.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 1. OBLIGATIONS IN GENERAL [1427 - 1543] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 2. INTERPRETATION OF OBLIGATIONS [1429 - 1451] ( Title 2 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 3. Conditional Obligations [1434 - 1442] ( Chapter 3 enacted 1872. ) 1442. A condition involving a forfeiture must be strictly interpreted against the party for whose benefit it is created. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the 30-day cure period, which contradicts the principle of allowing a reasonable opportunity to cure a breach before termination. The Law Explanation states that California Civil Code Section 1442 implies a right to cure a breach before a forfeiture can be enforced. Scraped Law Snippet 1, citing California Civil Code 1442, states that a condition involving a forfeiture must be strictly interpreted against the party for whose benefit it is created. By eliminating the cure period, the modified contract creates a forfeiture that is not interpreted against the party (XACCT) who benefits from it, thus contradicting the law.
